title: Silhouette portrait cutting
subtitle: Cutting a profile silhouette by hand from a traced or freehand outline
excerpt: A cut silhouette portrait requires a clean profile outline, black paper or card, and scissors or a scalpel. The technique dates from the eighteenth century and relies on a steady cutting hand and an understanding of which interior details can be removed to define a recognisable likeness. A well-cut silhouette is built from a single unbroken cut wherever possible.
sourceNotes: Profile silhouette cutting is documented in Victorian and Edwardian paper-craft manuals. See: Mary Badham, Paper Cutting and Pricking (1894 edition, public domain). The technique is also described in general accounts of silhouette portraiture from the 1700s onward.

glossaryTerms.definition: A silhouette cutting made from the side view of a subject, preserving the characteristic outline of the face and head.
A cut made within the interior of a silhouette to remove a pocket of background paper, defining features such as the nostril, ear canal, or eye socket.

content.text: silhouette depends on the quality of the outline. A traced photograph gives the most accurate profile. A freehand outline cut directly from observation takes more skill. Either method produces a recognisable portrait if the key features of the forehead, nose, lips, and chin are preserved faithfully.
Fine-point scissors and scalpels require full attention. Work on a stable cutting mat. Make small
entries before enlarging them to avoid overcutting.
Black paper or card, 80 to 130 gsm. Heavier card does not cut cleanly with scissors on tight curves; use a scalpel on card above 100 gsm.
White backing paper for mounting: the finished silhouette is glued to white or cream stock after cutting.
Photograph the subject in direct profile (the camera at eye height, the subject facing exactly 90° to the lens). Print at the desired finished size.
Lay tracing paper over the print. Trace only the outer profile line: the forehead, nose, lips, chin, jaw, neck, and hair outline. Do not trace interior features.
Transfer the traced outline to the back of the black paper by rubbing the reverse of the tracing with a soft pencil and redrawing over the lines.
Cut the outer profile with fine scissors or a scalpel. Rotate the paper rather than the tool through tight curves.
Make any inside cuts last: a small stab entry, then expand along the cut line. Common inside cuts: the gap between the lips, the nostril base, and the gap between the ear and the head.
Mount the finished silhouette on white or cream backing immediately; the cut edges are fragile before mounting.
